newsletter.markdown:
## Blogs

**[The Assault On Science Funding Continues](https://www.science.org/content/blog-post/assault-science-funding-continues)** — *Science.org*  
- Argues the Trump administration’s rhetoric about “gold standard science” and “reproducibility crisis” masks a deliberate effort to dismantle federal academic research funding (NIH, NSF) and redirect money to industry allies.  
- Highlights Scott Atlas’s call to abolish the NIH and Michael Kratsios’s proposal to shift funding from life sciences to robotics/AI.  
- Urges legal challenges, congressional oversight, and political mobilization rather than policy debate, which the author views as a distraction.

## News

**[STAT+: In win for RFK Jr., FDA advisory panel narrowly votes to allow compounding of unapproved peptides](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/23/fda-panel-okays-peptides-compound-pharmacies-bpc-157-kpv/?utm_campaign=rss)** — *STAT*  
- FDA advisory panel voted to permit compounding pharmacies to produce BPC-157 and KPV, two unapproved peptides with popular off-label use but no clinical efficacy data.  
- Decision aligns with RFK Jr.’s push for expanded access to unregulated therapeutics; narrow vote reflects division over safety vs. patient access.

## Papers & Preprints

**[Divergent Pyridine-to-Pyrazole Conversion via Carbon-Retentive Skeletal Editing](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42492024/)** — *PubMed*  
- New skeletal-editing method converts pyridines to pyrazoles while retaining all carbon atoms; potential for late-stage diversification of heterocycles.

**[Repurposing screens reveal a role for PKCη and NF1 in SARS-CoV-2 infection](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490662/)** — *PubMed*  
- Drug-repurposing screens identify protein kinase C eta (PKCη) and neurofibromin 1 (NF1) as host factors supporting SARS-CoV-2 infection.

**[Practical crystallography with a transmission electron microscope](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490642/)** — *PubMed*  
- Protocol for routine structure determination using transmission electron microcrystallography (MicroED), aimed at broader accessibility.

**[Identifying Cryptic Binding Sites with Mixed Solvent MD Simulation and SiteMap](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490529/)** — *PubMed*  
- Combines mixed-solvent molecular dynamics with SiteMap to reveal cryptic pockets for structure-based drug design.

**[Late-stage functionalization with strain-release warheads enables tunable covalent inhibition](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490492/)** — *PubMed*  
- Strain-release reactive groups allow late-stage installation of covalent warheads with adjustable reactivity for targeted inhibitor optimization.

**[Eukaryotic-like microtubules and dynamic instability of Asgard archaeal tubulins](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490446/)** — *PubMed*  
- Asgard archaeal tubulins form microtubules exhibiting dynamic instability, suggesting eukaryotic cytoskeleton machinery has deep archaeal roots.

**[Ligand Drift Rate Descriptor in Receptor Pocket: Molecular Docking Sampling for Differentiating Partial Agonists in Nuclear Receptors](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490415/)** — *PubMed*  
- Introduces a ligand drift rate metric from docking simulations to distinguish partial from full agonists at nuclear receptors.

**[Molecular Determinants of Ribosome Binding for the Antimicrobial Peptide Pdi1](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42490145/)** — *PubMed*  
- Structural and biochemical characterization of how the antimicrobial peptide Pdi1 engages the ribosome to inhibit translation.

## X / Twitter

**[𝐔.𝐒. 𝐒𝐜𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐒𝐮𝐦𝐦𝐢𝐭: Walking the streets of Kendall...](https://x.com/bradloncar/status/2080379247470772687)** — *@bradloncar*  
- On-the-ground impression from the U.S. Science Summit in Kendall Square (Cambridge, MA): “biotech version of SimCity”; tour led by Scott Kirsner, dinner with Greg Verdine.
newsletter.subject: Knowledge Secretary — 2026-07-24

youtube.markdown:
- Pure Science
    - [How Fast Is the Universe Really Expanding? | PODCAST: The Joy of Why](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Srcz66KUDbY) -- Quanta Magazine
        - Adam Riess traces his path from graduate student to Nobel laureate, detailing how Type Ia supernovae serve as standardizable candles for measuring cosmic distances via the distance ladder (Cepheid variables, tip of the red giant branch, and supernovae in the same host galaxies).
        - The 1998 discovery that distant supernovae were dimmer than expected revealed the universe’s expansion is accelerating, reviving Einstein’s cosmological constant as the leading explanation for dark energy within the ΛCDM model.
        - The Hubble tension now centers on a ~9% discrepancy: the local distance-ladder measurement yields H₀ ≈ 73 km/s/Mpc, while the CMB-inferred ΛCDM value is ≈ 67 km/s/Mpc — a difference that exceeds 5σ and cannot be explained by known systematic errors.
        - Riess outlines how new telescopes (JWST, Roman, ground-based ELTs) are targeting multiple independent distance anchors — Cepheids, TRGB, JAGB stars, and gravitational-wave standard sirens — to cross-check the distance ladder and test whether early-universe physics or new dark-energy dynamics resolve the tension.
        - The tension may indicate physics beyond ΛCDM, such as early dark energy, neutrino self-interactions, or a running Planck mass, but Riess emphasizes that exhaustive systematic checks on the distance-ladder side continue before invoking new cosmology.
